aliases: Fried Squid with Salt and Pepper

english: Fried Squid with Salt and Pepper

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general squid food product class; no specific salt-and-pepper fried squid entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers squid and cuttlefish as a category; no specific fried salt-and-pepper preparation code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for salt and pepper squid dish.

preparation_method: seasoned with salt and white pepper

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of salt and pepper squid is approximately 200 g, consisting of crispy fried squid pieces seasoned with salt and white pepper, served with a citrus mayonnaise dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Squid, mixed species, cooked, breaded and fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15150)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for breaded and fried squid (NDB 15150 / FDC 175168) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values represent squid pieces coated in cornstarch and deep-fried, which closely matches the Taiwanese salt-and-pepper squid preparation. The citrus mayonnaise accompaniment is served on the side and is not incorporated into the per-100g values, consistent with standard nutrient reporting for the main dish component. Sodium adjusted upward slightly to reflect seasoning with salt during preparation, consistent with the USDA value range for seasoned fried squid.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Taiwanese salt and pepper squid. GI estimated at 40 based on analogous deep-fried battered seafood and cornstarch-coated fried foods from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Cornstarch-battered fried foods typically fall in the GI 35–50 range (low category). GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 23.0) / 100 = 9.2, where carbohydrate per 200 g serving = 23.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~960 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid salt and pepper squid due to its high sodium content from seasoning and batter; a single 200 g serving may provide approximately 960 mg sodium, representing nearly 42% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from cornstarch batter with low GI; high fat from deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Salt and pepper squid has a low glycemic index (~40) due to the cornstarch batter, but the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~9.2) and the high fat content from deep-frying contributes to caloric density; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume in moderation and be mindful of total calorie and fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol (~198 mg per 100 g), sodium, and fat from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salt and pepper squid is high in dietary cholesterol and sodium and is deep-fried, increasing saturated and total fat intake; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption, and opt for grilled or steamed squid preparations when possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salt and Pepper Squid (椒盐鱿鱼 / 椒鹽魷魚) is a popular Taiwanese street food and restaurant dish consisting of fresh squid pieces coated in a light cornstarch batter, seasoned generously with salt and white pepper, and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish is typically garnished with fried garlic, chilli, and fresh basil leaves, which impart aromatic complexity. In Singapore, it is commonly served with a citrus mayonnaise dipping sauce, adding a creamy, tangy contrast to the crispy, savoury squid. The texture is characteristically crunchy on the outside with tender, slightly chewy squid inside. Nutritionally, salt and pepper squid provides a moderate amount of protein (~15 g per 100 g) from the squid, along with significant fat (~7.5 g per 100 g) from deep-frying. It is notably high in sodium (~480 mg per 100 g) due to seasoning, and contains moderate carbohydrates (~11.5 g per 100 g) from the cornstarch batter. Squid is a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus. The dish has a low glycemic index (~40) but a moderate glycemic load per serving. Clinically, the high sodium and cholesterol content make it a food to be consumed in moderation by individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for those with shellfish or mollusc allergies.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry exists for Taiwanese salt and pepper squid as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for breaded and fried squid (FDC 175168), which closely matches the preparation method. GI is estimated from analogous cornstarch-battered fried seafood in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source entry for breaded and fried squid. All other micronutrients with -1 values are not applicable here as they have been populated from the USDA source. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all populated from USDA FDC 175168. The citrus mayonnaise accompaniment is not included in per-100g nutrient values as it is a side condiment. The cuisine tag 'Burger' and 'Sandwich' from the input source appear to be erroneous field survey platform tags and have been disregarded. The dish is served at Riverside Buns - Boat Quay on field survey data at SGD 14.00. GI estimated at 40 (low category, ≤55 threshold) based on analogous deep-fried cornstarch-battered seafood from approved GI sources.

improvement_actions_taken: Populated all mandatory multilingual local names for Singaporean cuisine context (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Sourced nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC 175168 (breaded and fried squid) as closest approved source.
Estimated GI at 40 from analogous cornstarch-battered fried seafood per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg as -1 with explicit note that it is not reported in the source.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent.
Disregarded erroneous field survey cuisine tags ('Burger', 'Sandwich').
Set ontology match types exclusively to allowed values (exact, closest_parent, none).
Confirmed gi_category as 'low' for GI=40 per ≤55 threshold rule.
